w:r.w:t: One script. 13 phases. Everything from eBPF to Grafana.
Professional Edition · MIT License · GitHub: youruser/debian-trace-pro

DebianTrace Pro is a fully automated, production-grade Linux workstation setup designed for kernel engineers, performance analysts, and systems researchers. Built on Debian 12 (Bookworm), it transforms a stock system into a complete tracing and data analysis environment through a single, well-structured shell script. There is no manual configuration, no dependency hunting, and no guesswork — every tool is installed, pre-configured, and immediately usable.

The project addresses a real gap in the Linux ecosystem: while exceptional tracing tools exist — bpftrace, BCC, perf, ftrace, SystemTap — setting up a coherent, fully integrated environment across all of them is a time-consuming and error-prone process that can easily take days. DebianTrace Pro compresses that process to under one hour, delivering not just tools, but a complete professional workflow complete with shell aliases, tmux layouts, observability dashboards, and a data pipeline ready to ingest millions of trace events per second.
30–60 minutes on a typical broadband connection
eBPF · bpftrace · BCC (60+ tools) · perf · ftrace · SystemTap · Valgrind
Grafana · Prometheus · ClickHouse · Loki · Tempo (Docker Compose)
Python · R · Apache Spark 3.5 · JupyterLab · ClickHouse OLAP
oh-my-zsh · powerlevel10k · 50+ tracing aliases · tmux layouts
MIT — free to use, modify, and distribute

The result is an environment where a kernel engineer can open a terminal and immediately run a CPU flamegraph, trace every file open on the system, stream eBPF events into ClickHouse, and visualise the output in Grafana — all within minutes of arriving at the workstation.

Kernel tracing and performance analysis are among the most powerful disciplines in systems engineering. A practitioner who can fluently use perf, bpftrace, and BCC can diagnose in minutes what a standard monitoring stack would take hours or days to surface — the exact function that caused a latency spike, which process is responsible for a surge in I/O, or where memory is leaking at the byte level.

Yet the barrier to entry remains high. A functioning eBPF environment requires the right kernel headers, a matching BCC build, clang/LLVM at the correct version, and libbpf properly linked. Adding Grafana for visualisation means standing up Prometheus, configuring scrapers, and connecting data sources. Adding ClickHouse for event analytics means yet another installation chain. Each component is individually well-documented but there is no single, maintained reference for assembling all of them into a coherent, working whole on a modern Debian system.

DebianTrace Pro is that reference, made executable. It is a single, auditable Bash script that walks through thirteen carefully ordered phases, each building on the last. It installs kernel headers and BPF infrastructure before attempting to build eBPF tooling. It installs Docker before deploying the observability stack. It tunes sysctl kernel parameters only after the base system is stable. Every phase is logged, every failure is caught and reported, and flags like --minimal allow users to scope the installation to their specific needs.

The design philosophy is that infrastructure should be invisible. An engineer arriving at a DebianTrace Pro system should be able to type bt-syscalls and immediately see live eBPF syscall data, type obs-up and have Grafana ready in seconds, or run perf-flame and open a flamegraph SVG without remembering a single flag. The tool should get out of the way so the actual analysis work can begin.
✔ All tools installed and linked in one run
✔ 50+ production-ready shell aliases, ready on first login
✔ Grafana + Prometheus + ClickHouse + Loki + Tempo, pre-configured
✗ Raw perf output requires manual processing
✗ No data pipeline for trace events
✔ ClickHouse ready to ingest millions of events/sec
✔ BPF JIT, perf paranoid, tracefs, sysctl all pre-optimised
✔ Pinned versions, idempotent flags, install log for every run
DebianTrace Pro is built for practitioners who work at or near the kernel boundary and need their environment to keep up with them. Specifically, it targets:
Writing, debugging, or optimising kernel code, drivers, or BPF programs. The full eBPF C, Go, and Rust development chains are ready out of the box.
Diagnosing CPU bottlenecks, memory pressure, scheduler latency, lock contention, and I/O wait. perf, FlameGraph, BCC, and bpftrace are pre-wired together.
Correlating system-level events with application behaviour. The Grafana + Prometheus + Loki + Tempo observability stack provides infrastructure-level telemetry that complements APM data.
Auditing system call patterns, capability usage, and network behaviour at the kernel level using eBPF programs and bpftrace one-liners without modifying target processes.
Processing and querying large volumes of trace data. Apache Spark, ClickHouse, JupyterLab, and a full Python scientific stack are ready for the analytical workload.
The stack can be understood as three interconnected layers:

Linux kernel instrumentation points: kprobes, uprobes, tracepoints, PMU hardware counters, ftrace, and eBPF programs that run in the kernel context. Tools in this layer (bpftrace, BCC, perf, ftrace, SystemTap) have sub-microsecond overhead and direct access to kernel data structures.

Data export infrastructure that moves trace events from kernel to user-space. Prometheus node_exporter exposes host metrics. Loki collects structured logs. Tempo receives distributed traces via OTLP. ClickHouse provides a native TCP ingest path for high-volume event streams.

Tools for querying, transforming, and visualising the collected data. Grafana provides real-time dashboards. JupyterLab with Python and R supports exploratory and statistical analysis. Apache Spark enables processing of trace files that exceed available memory.
Installation requires a clean Debian 12 Bookworm system, root access, and an internet connection. The single command below handles the rest:

Optional flags allow scoping the installation: --headless for unattended CI runs, --skip-gui to omit the XFCE desktop on servers, and --minimal for a lean install that covers only the core tracing tools without the data science and observability stacks.

After installation, a reboot is recommended to apply all kernel parameter changes. From the first login, every tracing alias, the full observability stack command (obs-up), and the complete documentation at ~/TRACE-QUICKREF.md are immediately available.
